findings.summary: execute/pymdp/__init__.py imports from analysis/ at module-init time, creating a downstream-to-upstream coupling
evidence: Line 27 of execute/pymdp/__init__.py: `from analysis.pymdp.visualizer import PyMDPVisualizer, create_visualizer, save_all_visualizations`
execute/ (step 12) is a downstream consumer of analysis/ (step 16) — violating pipeline step order
analysis/ does not import from execute/, making this a one-way but incorrect direction dependency
findings.suggestion: Move PyMDPVisualizer into execute/pymdp/ or a shared visualization utility; do not pull step-16 (analysis) code into step-12 (execute).

findings.summary: visualization/processor.py imports from analysis/analyzer.py, creating a lateral peer coupling that blurs ownership
evidence: Line 86 of visualization/processor.py: `from analysis.analyzer import parse_matrix_data, generate_matrix_visualizations`
Step 8 (visualization) importing from step 16 (analysis) violates pipeline step-ordering direction
findings.suggestion: Move shared matrix analysis utilities into a bounded shared module (e.g., gnn/math_utils.py or a new shared/matrix.py) that both visualization and analysis can import without layer violations.

findings.summary: render/pymdp/pymdp_renderer.py embeds a hard import of src.execute.pymdp inside a code-generation template string, coupling render to execute at code-gen time
evidence: Line 440 in pymdp_renderer.py (inside a generated script template): `from src.execute.pymdp import execute_pymdp_simulation`
This import is inside a Python string that gets written to output files, so it embeds an execute-layer dependency into rendered artifacts from the render layer
findings.suggestion: Generated scripts should be self-contained or reference a stable public API rather than importing from render/'s sibling execute/.

findings.summary: utils/ is a 50-file god-module that every other module imports from, creating a high-blast-radius hub with no internal layering
evidence: utils/__init__.py re-exports 130+ symbols from 14+ sub-modules in a single flat namespace
grep finds 130 total `from utils.` import occurrences across 75 files

utils/ contains: logging (6 files), config (2 files), pipeline orchestration (5 files), dependency management (3 files), testing utilities, argument parsing, base classes — entirely different domains co-located
Any change to any utils sub-module propagates blast radius to all 75 importing files
findings.suggestion: Decompose utils/ into purpose-bounded sub-packages: utils/logging/, utils/config/, utils/pipeline/, utils/testing/. The existing utils/logging/ subdir is a start but is incomplete and inconsistently used.

findings.summary: src/sapf/ is a permanent compatibility shim with no active external callers outside tests, persisting without a clear sunset plan
evidence: sapf/__init__.py re-exports everything from audio/sapf/ and documents itself as a shim with dual absolute+relative import fallback
The shim uses a try/except import chain (audio.sapf, then ..audio.sapf) that obscures the canonical path
CLAUDE.md acknowledges this is a compatibility shim but lists no external requirement that makes it necessary — described only as keeping `import sapf` working
findings.suggestion: If no external tool depends on `import sapf` (vs `import audio.sapf`), remove the shim and update the few callers. If an external tool does require it, document the external caller explicitly.

findings.summary: log_step_start/success/warning/error are defined or inlined in 17 distinct locations rather than owned by one canonical source
evidence: grep finds 68 total def log_step_start/success/warning/error lines across the codebase
At least 9 files define inline fallback lambdas: `def log_step_start(logger, msg): logger.info(f'🚀 {msg}')`
5 different module-level implementations exist: utils/logging_utils.py, utils/logging/logging_utils.py, utils/structured_logging.py, utils/step_logging.py, utils/diagnostic_logging.py
utils/step_logging.py is marked DEPRECATED but is still the primary import target via step_logging fallbacks
findings.suggestion: Designate one canonical module (utils/logging/logging_utils.py) as the single source. Remove all fallback inline redefinitions by fixing the import chain so they are never needed.

findings.summary: utils/pipeline_template.py contains two nearly-identical factory functions (create_standard_pipeline_script and create_standardized_pipeline_script) with duplicated bodies of ~100 lines each
evidence: create_standard_pipeline_script defined at line 56, create_standardized_pipeline_script at line 376
Both functions implement the same argument-parse → logging-setup → module-function-call pattern with minor variations
Step scripts import only create_standardized_pipeline_script but both live in the file
The file also contains a second module docstring starting at line 178 indicating file was assembled by merging two files without cleanup
findings.suggestion: Delete create_standard_pipeline_script (unused by step scripts) and remove the second module docstring block. Consolidate into one factory with parameterized behavior.

findings.summary: Pipeline validation logic is split across two separate files (utils/pipeline_validator.py and pipeline/pipeline_validator.py) with overlapping responsibilities
evidence: utils/pipeline_validator.py (227 lines) defines validate_step_prerequisites and validate_pipeline_step_sequence
pipeline/pipeline_validator.py (445 lines) defines the same function names
main.py imports from utils.pipeline_validator; pipeline/ module uses its own copy
Ownership of validation logic is split between two co-equal packages
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into pipeline/pipeline_validator.py (the domain-appropriate location) and re-export from utils if backwards compatibility is required.

findings.summary: utils/__init__.py imports and exports validate_config twice from two different source modules, with the second import silently overwriting the first in the namespace
evidence: Line 103 in utils/__init__.py: `from .config_loader import ... validate_config ...`
Line 222 in utils/__init__.py: `from .configuration import ... validate_config ...`
These have different signatures: config_loader's takes a dict, configuration's takes an optional schema_name string
PerformanceTracker is similarly imported twice: line 37 from logging_utils, line 109 from performance_tracker
findings.suggestion: Namespace-qualify both: e.g., expose as validate_pipeline_config and validate_schema_config. Fix PerformanceTracker duplicate similarly.

findings.summary: pytest and 5 pytest plugins are listed as core (non-optional) dependencies, making them mandatory for all users including production deployments
evidence: Core `dependencies` block includes: pytest>=6.0.0, pytest-timeout>=2.1.0, pytest-asyncio>=0.23.0, pytest-cov>=3.0.0, pytest-json-report>=1.5.0, pytest-xdist>=2.5.0
These also appear again in `[project.optional-dependencies] dev` with different version pins (pytest-timeout>=2.1.0 and pytest-xdist>=3.0.0 duplicated)
pytest-timeout and pytest-xdist appear with conflicting lower-bound versions (2.5.0 vs 3.0.0 for xdist) between core and dev extras
findings.suggestion: Move all pytest packages to the `dev` optional-dependencies group. Remove the duplicate entries. Resolve the version lower-bound conflict for pytest-xdist.

findings.summary: Heavy JAX ecosystem (jax[cpu], jaxlib, flax, optax) are mandatory core deps despite being used only in optional render/execute backends
evidence: Core deps include: jax[cpu]>=0.4.0, jaxlib>=0.4.0, flax>=0.7.0, optax>=0.1.0 — these are multi-hundred-MB installs
Usage is confined to render/jax/ and execute/jax/ which are optional backends (guarded by try/except in the pipeline)

An active-inference optional extra also re-declares jax[cpu] and jaxlib, and a probabilistic-programming extra does the same — indicating these were intentionally factored as optional but were left in core
findings.suggestion: Move jax[cpu], jaxlib, flax, optax to the `jax` optional extra. Pipeline step scripts already handle missing JAX gracefully via try/except.

findings.summary: Core deps pin inferactively-pymdp>=0.0.7 while the active-inference optional extra pins pymdp==0.0.1, creating a version conflict when both are installed
evidence: Line 64: `"inferactively-pymdp>=0.0.7"` in core dependencies (inferactively-pymdp is the distribution name for the pymdp package)
Line 125 in `active-inference` extra: `"pymdp==0.0.1"` — pinned to an older version incompatible with >=0.0.7
Comment on line 125 says 'Pinned to match main dependencies' but 0.0.1 < 0.0.7, so this is internally contradictory
findings.suggestion: Remove pymdp==0.0.1 from the active-inference extra (the core dep already covers it). If a specific version is needed for that extra, align the pin to >=0.0.7.

findings.summary: gradio, discopy, altair, and plotly are mandatory core dependencies despite being used only by optional GUI and visualization backends
evidence: Core deps include gradio>=4.44.1 (GUI library, 200MB+), discopy[matrix]>=1.0.0, altair>=5.0.0, plotly>=6.2.0
gradio is only imported in src/gui/ subdirectories (gui_1, gui_2, gui_3) under try/except guards
altair has zero non-test direct imports; all 15 plotly/altair usages are in visualization optional contexts
Both appear again under visualization optional extra, confirming they were intended as optional
findings.suggestion: Move gradio to the gui optional extra, discopy to an active-inference or categorical extra, altair and plotly to the visualization extra.

findings.summary: utils/ has 50 files mixing logging, config, pipeline orchestration, dependency management, testing, and argument parsing with no sub-package boundaries
evidence: `ls src/utils/ | wc -l` = 52 items (50 Python files + subdirs)

Files include: 6 logging variants (logging_utils.py, structured_logging.py, step_logging.py, diagnostic_logging.py, visual_logging.py, logging/logging_utils.py), 2 config systems (config_loader.py, configuration.py), 5 pipeline-specific files (pipeline.py, pipeline_monitor.py, pipeline_planner.py, pipeline_validator.py, pipeline_template.py, pipeline_dependencies.py), dependency management (dependency_validator.py, dependency_audit.py, dependency_installer.py, dependency_manager.py)
The existing utils/logging/ sub-package is only partially adopted — most consumers still import from utils.logging_utils or utils.structured_logging directly
findings.suggestion: Create utils/logging/, utils/config/, utils/pipeline/, utils/dependency/ sub-packages and migrate files. The logging/ sub-package is already partially started. Make utils/__init__.py a stable re-export surface from these sub-packages.

findings.summary: src/pipeline_step_template.py sits at the root src/ level despite being functionally identical to src/pipeline/pipeline_step_template.py
evidence: src/pipeline_step_template.py imports from utils.pipeline_template and re-exposes the same pipeline step template pattern
src/pipeline/pipeline_step_template.py exists and serves the same purpose
grep shows only one test imports from the root-level copy: `from src.pipeline import pipeline_step_template`
The root-level copy has low fan-in (1 caller) and shares concern entirely with src/pipeline/
findings.suggestion: Delete src/pipeline_step_template.py and update the one test to import from src.pipeline.pipeline_step_template.

findings.summary: log_step_start/success/warning/error defined in at least 8 distinct locations with no canonical authority
evidence: grep finds 18 files defining def log_step_start
step_logging.py is explicitly marked DEPRECATED but is the canonical import source for base_processor.py and many processors
execute/processor.py defines inline fallback: def log_step_start(logger, msg): logger.info(f'🚀 {msg}')
gnn/processor.py defines the same four inline fallbacks verbatim
utils/logging_utils.py and utils/logging/logging_utils.py are parallel files with overlapping definitions
findings.suggestion: Designate exactly one canonical module (e.g. utils/logging/logging_utils.py) and remove all duplicate definitions. Delete step_logging.py shim entirely after migrating callers.

findings.summary: ErrorSeverity defined as two incompatible Enum classes in sibling modules within the same package
evidence: error_handling.py: class ErrorSeverity(Enum) with values LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH/CRITICAL
error_recovery.py: class ErrorSeverity(Enum) with values INFO/WARNING/ERROR/CRITICAL — different member names
utils/__init__.py imports ErrorSeverity from BOTH modules in the same try block, causing the second import to shadow the first at the package namespace
findings.suggestion: Merge into a single ErrorSeverity enum in one module; delete the duplicate. Audit all callers to confirm which value set they expect.

findings.summary: PerformanceTracker class defined in 5 different files with divergent interfaces
evidence: grep shows 'class PerformanceTracker' in 5 files
utils/performance_tracker.py has track_operation() context manager and get_summary()
utils/diagnostic_logging.py has start_operation()/stop_operation() imperative API
utils/logging/logging_utils.py defines a fallback stub with only track_operation and get_summary
Callers must guess which class they received
findings.suggestion: Keep only utils/performance_tracker.py; convert all other definitions to imports. Remove fallback stubs that duplicate the interface.

findings.summary: setup_step_logging defined in 9 distinct places creating an archipelago of divergent implementations
evidence: grep 'def setup_step_logging' returns 9 matches across 7 files
utils/pipeline.py setup_step_logging wraps utils.logging_utils.setup_step_logging with try/except, adding a layer that is itself identical to the wrapped function on the happy path
health_check.py defines a local def setup_step_logging inside a try/except fallback block
findings.suggestion: Single canonical definition in utils/logging/logging_utils.py. Remove all other definitions. Update imports project-wide.

findings.summary: find_gnn_files silently swallows all file-read exceptions with bare except/continue
evidence: Lines 33-40: try: ... except Exception: continue — no logging, no error propagation
Callers receive a silently-truncated file list with no indication that files were skipped
findings.suggestion: At minimum log a warning with the file path and exception before continuing. Consider collecting errors and returning them alongside the file list.

findings.summary: pipeline/execution.py silently swallows mkdir failures with only a warnings-list entry, never propagated to caller
evidence: Lines 58-62: except Exception: results['warnings'].append(f'Could not ensure output dir: {output_dir}') then continues
Subsequent writes will fail with harder-to-diagnose errors rather than a clear early failure
findings.suggestion: Fail fast if output_dir cannot be created, or at minimum log at WARNING level and stop attempting file-based steps that require the directory.

findings.summary: Pipeline functions mix three incompatible error contracts: raise Exception, return False/None, and return dict with 'success' key
evidence: pipeline/execution.py: run_pipeline() returns dict with 'success' key; validate_pipeline_config() returns bool
base_processor.py: process() returns ProcessingResult dataclass; process_single_file() returns bool
unified_parser.py: parse_file() raises ParseError and FileNotFoundError
shared_functions.py: find_gnn_files() returns list (errors swallowed); validate_file_paths() returns Tuple[bool, List[str]]
No consistent contract allows callers to handle errors uniformly
findings.suggestion: Adopt a single error contract for each layer: parsers raise domain exceptions; processors return a typed Result object; CLI/script entry points catch and exit. Eliminate the dict-with-success-key pattern.

findings.summary: PipelineErrorHandler.error_context context manager silently drops non-critical exceptions (exit_code==2 path has bare pass)
evidence: Lines 220-236: except Exception as e: ... elif exit_code == 2: pass  # Allow caller to decide
The exception object e is destroyed; caller has no way to inspect what happened or act on it
StandardizedErrorHandler.error_context (standardized_error_handling.py) logs a warning but also silently continues
findings.suggestion: Yield the error context or re-raise as a non-fatal domain exception so callers can observe and handle recoverable errors.

findings.summary: validate_pipeline_config catches all exceptions and returns False with no logging
evidence: Lines 123-129: def validate_pipeline_config(config: dict) -> bool: try: ... except Exception: return False
Any TypeError, AttributeError, or real bug is hidden from the caller
findings.suggestion: Remove the bare except; a dict membership check does not raise exceptions under normal circumstances. If guarding against malformed input is intentional, at minimum log the exception.

findings.summary: Multi-paragraph docstrings on trivial 2-5 line wrapper functions throughout utils/
evidence: utils/pipeline.py setup_step_logging: 8-line docstring with Args/Returns sections for a 3-line function that calls _setup_step_logging or falls back to logging.getLogger
utils/fallback.py FallbackArgumentParser.parse_step_arguments: 7-line docstring for a function that instantiates a DefaultArgs inner class with three hardcoded fields
standardized_error_handling.py handle_dependency_error: full Args/Returns docblock including description of what 'True' means for a function whose body is always 'return True'
findings.suggestion: Replace bloated docstrings on trivial functions with a single-line docstring. Reserve Args/Returns blocks for non-obvious signatures.

findings.summary: Comments that restate the code verbatim are pervasive throughout utils/ and pipeline/
evidence: error_handling.py line 117: '# Classify error severity and recovery strategy' directly above call to self._classify_error(error, category)
error_handling.py line 121: '# Extract error context' above error_context = context or {}
error_handling.py line 127: '# Create structured error' above pipeline_error = PipelineError(...)
pipeline/execution.py line 52: '# Normalize inputs' above if pipeline_data is None: pipeline_data = {}
error_handling.py line 283: '# Generate breakdowns' above a for-loop over errors
findings.suggestion: Remove comments that simply name the next line. Keep only comments that explain WHY, not WHAT.

findings.summary: The four-function inline fallback block (log_step_start/success/warning/error as emoji-prefixed logger calls) is copy-pasted verbatim across at least 6 processor files
evidence: gnn/processor.py lines 22-25: def log_step_start(logger, msg): logger.info(f'🚀 {msg}') ... (identical in all files)
execute/processor.py lines 29-32: same four functions verbatim
These are never differentiated; every copy is identical
utils/step_logging.py already provides these exact fallbacks and is always importable — making the inline copies unnecessary
findings.suggestion: Remove all inline fallback definitions; import unconditionally from utils.step_logging which is always importable per its own design.

findings.summary: pipeline/execution.py deliberately sleeps to make performance tests pass rather than implementing real work
evidence: Lines 100-106: '# Ensure actual runtime meets or exceeds conservative estimate' followed by time.sleep(min(remaining, 5 * total_estimated_time))
Comment on line 65: 'This makes performance tests compare favorably against conservative estimates'
execute_pipeline_step returns a hardcoded StepExecutionResult(success=True, duration=0.1, output='Step N executed successfully') — the function body contains no actual logic
findings.suggestion: Replace the stub run_pipeline with the real orchestration logic. Delete the sleep. Fix performance tests to assert against real behavior.

findings.summary: utils/__init__.py is a 435-line mega-re-export surface that imports from 15+ submodules, obscuring module structure and creating a god-namespace
evidence: 435 lines; imports from logging_utils, argument_utils, resource_manager, error_recovery, pipeline_monitor, pipeline_validator, pipeline_planner, dependency_validator, config_loader, performance_tracker, step_logging, base_processor, venv_utils, system_utils, test_utils, pipeline, error_handling, structured_logging, configuration, dependency_audit — 20 source modules
Exports test infrastructure (TestRunner, TestCategory, get_test_coverage) alongside core pipeline utilities
Single try/except wraps all 20 module imports; one broken module silently degrades all utilities
152 occurrences of 'from utils import' or 'from utils.' across 84 files — callers import heterogeneous concerns from the same namespace
findings.suggestion: Expose only genuinely cross-cutting utilities (setup_step_logging, log_step_*) from __init__.py. Move domain-specific groups (test_utils, configuration, dependency_audit) to their own importable subpackages. Callers should import from specific submodules.

findings.summary: setup_step_logging passes through 3-4 wrapper hops before reaching logging.getLogger with no policy added at any layer
evidence: utils/pipeline.py: setup_step_logging calls _setup_step_logging from logging_utils (imported inside try)
utils/logging_utils.py: setup_step_logging calls PipelineLogger.get_logger()
PipelineLogger.get_logger calls logging.getLogger() and optionally adds a StreamHandler
No layer adds retry, metrics, context enrichment, or policy — pure forwarding all the way down
utils/step_logging.py adds another hop: re-exports from utils.logging.logging_utils (also imported via try/except)
findings.suggestion: Collapse to a single setup_step_logging function that calls logging.getLogger directly with the formatter. Eliminate the intermediate PipelineLogger class wrapper which provides no meaningful policy.

findings.summary: StandardizedErrorHandler is a pass-through wrapper over PipelineErrorHandler with no additional policy
evidence: StandardizedErrorHandler.__init__ creates a PipelineErrorHandler and stores it as self.pipeline_error_handler
Every method (handle_dependency_error, handle_file_operation_error, handle_validation_error) calls self.pipeline_error_handler.create_error() then self.pipeline_error_handler.handle_error()
The only 'policy' is mapping Python exception types to ErrorCategory — which is equally appropriate inside PipelineErrorHandler._classify_error
ErrorRecoveryManager in error_recovery.py provides a third, parallel error-handling class with yet another interface
findings.suggestion: Merge the auto-categorize logic from _categorize_error into PipelineErrorHandler._classify_error and delete StandardizedErrorHandler. Keep one error handler class.

findings.summary: Two parallel configuration subsystems (config_loader.py dataclasses and configuration.py ConfigurationManager) serve the same purpose with no clear ownership boundary
evidence: config_loader.py defines GNNPipelineConfig, PipelineConfig, TypeCheckerConfig, OntologyConfig, LLMConfig, WebsiteConfig, SetupConfig, SAPFConfig, ModelConfig plus load_config, save_config, validate_config
configuration.py defines ConfigurationManager class with init_config, get_config, set_config, validate_config, get_pipeline_config, get_step_config — duplicating config_loader semantics
utils/__init__.py exports validate_config from both modules in the same namespace, causing the second import to silently shadow the first
No documentation explains which system is authoritative
findings.suggestion: Pick one approach: either typed dataclass config (config_loader pattern) or a runtime ConfigurationManager. Delete the other. Fix the shadowing collision in __init__.py immediately.

findings.summary: log_step_* functions are imported via three different paths with inconsistent fallback stubs duplicated across dozens of modules
evidence: gnn/processor.py imports from utils.pipeline_template with a try/except fallback that redefines all four log_step_* stubs inline
execute/processor.py imports from utils.step_logging with identical four-function fallback block
analysis/processor.py repeats same try/except stub block from utils.pipeline_template
utils/step_logging.py is marked DEPRECATED and itself imports from utils.logging.logging_utils with yet another fallback
utils/logging_utils.py (root-level) re-exports from utils.logging/logging_utils — three modules for one concern
At least three canonical import paths exist: utils.pipeline_template, utils.step_logging, utils (via __init__), and utils.logging.logging_utils
findings.suggestion: Designate utils.logging.logging_utils as the single canonical source. Remove step_logging.py shim after updating all callers. Eliminate inline stub fallbacks in individual processor files — if the canonical module is absent the step cannot function anyway.

findings.summary: render/processor.py mutates sys.path at processing time and tries two import spellings inside a nested try/except to locate its sibling modules
evidence: Lines 185-210: inside process_render(), inserts both src_path and project_root into sys.path then tries 'from gnn.pomdp_extractor import ...' and falls back to 'from src.gnn.pomdp_extractor import ...' — a boundary that should be resolved at package install time, not call time
This dual-spelling pattern is inside the hot path called per GNN file, not at module load time
findings.suggestion: Fix the package structure so that gnn and render are importable without path surgery. A single top-level sys.path.insert in each numbered step script (already done there) is sufficient; processor-level path mutation is unexpected and hides misconfiguration.

findings.summary: pipeline/execution.py is a non-functional stub whose run_pipeline() sleeps to satisfy timing tests rather than executing real pipeline steps
evidence: Lines 92-106: iterates planned_steps and records success=True with duration=0.0 for every step without invoking any real step logic
Line 105-106: '# Sleep the remaining time to satisfy tests comparing estimate vs actual' followed by time.sleep(min(remaining, 5 * total_estimated_time))
execute_pipeline_step() at line 154 returns StepExecutionResult(success=True, duration=0.1, output='Step X executed successfully') as a hardcoded stub
Comment says 'In practice, this would import and execute the actual step' — this code has been shipped as-is
findings.suggestion: Either implement run_pipeline() to actually invoke the numbered step scripts via subprocess or dynamic import, or remove it entirely and have callers use main.py directly. The sleep-to-satisfy-tests pattern is a test strategy defect as much as a seam defect; tests should not be written against timing of a stub.

findings.summary: Identical four-function log_step fallback blocks are copy-pasted verbatim into at least five processor files
evidence: gnn/processor.py lines 22-25: def log_step_start(logger, msg): logger.info(f'🚀 {msg}') repeated identically
execute/processor.py lines 29-32: exact same four-function block
analysis/processor.py lines 21-24: exact same block
The stubs use emoji literals hardcoded, diverging from the canonical formatted strings in utils/logging/logging_utils.py
findings.suggestion: Define a single module-private fallback (e.g. utils/_fallback_logging.py) and import it in the except branch. Given that step_logging.py already exists for this purpose, route all fallbacks through it.

findings.summary: utils/pipeline_template.py contains both live production logic and unreachable template/example functions that reference undefined helpers
evidence: Lines 178-286: a second module-level docstring appears mid-file ('Standardized Pipeline Module Function Template'), followed by re-imports already done at the top, and functions like standard_module_function and process_single_file that call from .shared_functions import find_gnn_files — relative imports that cannot resolve in a utils/ package
process_single_file at line 273 returns True unconditionally: 'This is a template — specific modules should override this'
Lines 184-191 contain commented-out imports, indicating abandoned dead code
findings.suggestion: Remove the template/example code from the production file. If examples are needed, put them in a docs/ or examples/ directory, not in the live utils package.

findings.summary: utils/pipeline_template.py exposes two nearly-identical factory functions (create_standard_pipeline_script and create_standardized_pipeline_script) with overlapping logic and step-name special-cases hardcoded inside
evidence: create_standard_pipeline_script (line 56) and create_standardized_pipeline_script (line 376) both produce run_* closures that handle argument parsing, path defaults, and module invocation

Both functions contain step-specific conditionals: 'if step_name == "11_render.py"' and 'if step_name in ["11_render.py", "12_execute.py"]' — step names encoded as string literals inside a shared utility
The comment in create_standard_pipeline_script says steps 9 and 10 but the condition names steps 11 and 12, showing the code drifted from its own comments
Numbered scripts use create_standardized_pipeline_script (the newer form) while the older form still exists and is referenced by pipeline_step_template.py
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into one factory. Move step-specific defaults (default target dir, recursive flag) into a per-step configuration table or into the step scripts themselves, not inside the shared utility.

findings.summary: process_analysis() in analysis/processor.py handles both statistical analysis of GNN source files and post-simulation result loading, path construction, and cross-framework aggregation in a single 150-line function
evidence: Lines 145-170: discovers and statistically analyzes .md GNN files

Lines 182-249: navigates to output/12_execute_output via relative parent path manipulation ('output_dir.parent / "12_execute_output"'), loads JSON summaries, invokes visualize_simulation_results, and iterates again over gnn_files to run per-model post-simulation analysis
Two distinct outer loops over gnn_files with different concerns inside the same function body
Hard-coded path segment '12_execute_output' couples analysis step to execution step's output directory naming
findings.suggestion: Split into two functions: one for GNN source analysis, one for post-simulation analysis. Pass the execution output directory as an explicit parameter rather than deriving it via relative path from the analysis output dir.

findings.summary: Multiple integration tests contain assertions that are logically always true, providing zero coverage signal
evidence: test_pipeline_integration.py line 85: 'assert result is not None or result is None' — always True
test_pipeline_integration.py line 102: 'assert result is not None or output_dir.exists()' — always True (output_dir was just mkdir'd)
test_report_generation.py line 143: 'assert result is True or result is False' — always True
test_audio_integration.py line 117: 'assert result is True or result is False  # Both valid outcomes'
test_report_integration.py has three identical result-is-not-None-or-is-None assertions
findings.suggestion: Replace tautological assertions with explicit contract verification: check that success returns True and that expected output files exist, OR mark the test as xfail/skip if the outcome is genuinely unknown. Tests that cannot fail provide no regression protection.

findings.summary: Tests in test_pipeline_performance.py and test_pipeline_overall.py drive pipeline/execution.py which is a no-op stub, giving false confidence about pipeline correctness
evidence: test_pipeline_performance.py calls process_gnn_directory expecting result['status'] == 'SUCCESS', but pipeline/execution.py's run_pipeline always returns success=True with no real work
execute_pipeline_step in execution.py is a hardcoded stub returning duration=0.1 and success=True unconditionally
The performance thresholds in THRESHOLDS dict are never actually validated against real processing — the stub completes instantly then sleeps to 'satisfy' timing tests
test_parallel_processing asserts result['processed_files'] OR result['files'] exists without verifying actual file processing occurred
findings.suggestion: Either implement real pipeline execution and test against it, or explicitly mark these as 'smoke tests' that only verify the API surface. Remove the timing-sleep hack; if real performance tests are needed they should exercise real processing paths.

findings.summary: 576 occurrences of safe_to_fail or pytest.skip across 46 test files, many wrapping core-path tests that silently skip on ImportError
evidence: test_pipeline_overall.py: every test in TestPipelineModuleComprehensive and TestPipelineFunctionality is marked safe_to_fail and contains try/except ImportError: pytest.skip — the entire class can silently pass with zero assertions exercised
576 total safe_to_fail/skip occurrences found across 46 files out of ~90 test files (>50% of test files)
Tests for PipelineOrchestrator, PipelineStep, get_pipeline_config — core types — are all skippable on ImportError
No CI enforcement distinguishes between 'skipped because optional dep absent' and 'skipped because core module broken'
findings.suggestion: Separate genuinely optional tests (those requiring external services or optional packages like Julia) from tests of always-present core modules. Core module tests should fail hard, not skip, when imports fail. Add a CI job that fails if skip count exceeds a threshold.
